summary: The best way to address the concerns of China's trade partners is to let the Chinese people live better.

title: The Case for Higher U.S. Rates
summary: Hormuz may be opening up, but the combination of robust growth and stable-to-accelerating underlying inflation suggests that the previous bias to lowering rates was a mistake.

title: Yes, Living Standards Have Grown Slower in Northwest Europe than in the U.S.
summary: But what that means for relative income levels is much less clear.

title: Russia's Underwhelming Oil Revenue Windfall
summary: The latest data show that the increase in energy prices since the start of the conflict with Iran has done relatively little to improve the finances of the Russian government or its military industry.

title: The U.S. Job Market is (Still) Inflationary
summary: Wage growth is now accelerating slightly, supporting the quickening of "supercore" service price increases. Plus: more on the health care wage slowdown mystery.

title: The Growth Impulse from the Data Center Boom
summary: There is much less than meets the eye, although the official numbers may also be undercounting some of what is happening.

title: "Look Through" the Hormuz Shock if You Want. U.S. Inflation is Still Running Hot.
summary: A wide range of underlying measures indicates that prices are rising too fast to be consistent with the Fed's alleged 2% yearly target. Plus: explaining the CPI-PCE gap.

title: Is U.S. Wage Growth Slowing Or Not?
summary: Something strange is happening with wages in healthcare and private education.

title: Russia's Iran War Windfall in Perspective
summary: As long as financial sanctions and export controls remain in place, the aggressor's gains from higher oil and gas prices will be limited.

title: The Fed is Misreading the Inflation Risks
summary: Inflation was getting worse *before the war* across a broad range of categories. Yet Fed officials are still blaming "one-time things".

title: Markets Are Still Sanguine About the Oil Outlook
summary: Prior oil shocks in 1973 and 1979 involved far larger price increases than what we have seen so far. Yet this shock involves a hit to volumes that is unprecedented outside of the pandemic.

title: If You Thought the Inflation Outlook Was Bad Before...
summary: Quantifying its size is impossible given how little we know about when and how this will end, but the direction of the impact is obvious. The big question is whether long-term beliefs will shift.

title: Tariffs and "International Payments Problems"
summary: It has long been accepted that tariffs are an acceptable tool for managing balance of payments issues. The real question is whether they would be useful for the U.S. in 2026.

title: Arbitrary U.S. Tariffs Are Gone. What's Next?
summary: The Supreme Court has ruled that the President cannot use "emergency" authority to impose tariffs at will. Inflation is still going to be a problem.

title: Australia Is Raising Rates. Why Not the Fed?
summary: The U.S. economy is accelerating, yet policymakers are still unreasonably concerned about downside risks.

title: Russian War Spending May be Maxed Out
summary: The official budget deficit surged last year, as did off-balance sheet military spending via the banking system and unpaid bills. That might be tough to sustain, especially with lower oil revenues.

title: Is the Market Underpricing the Risk of Fed Hikes?
summary: The incoming data are undermining the case for lowering rates, which means that the 0% implied probability of hikes may be too low. And that is before considering backlash to Trump's attacks.

title: Is Japan "Normal" Again?
summary: After decades of trying, it looks as if inflation and nominal income growth have finally reset to a new, faster baseline. That explains the actions in bond yields, and why the yen has not appreciated.

title: U.S. Wages Still Rising Briskly, Employment Holding Up, and Inflation Still Sticky (Probably)
summary: Look through the noise attributable to the government shutdown, and little has changed over the past six months.

title: The Fed Submits?
summary: America's central bankers have, in the aggregate, lowered the "appropriate" path for interest rates despite an improved growth outlook and a worsening inflation picture. There is no good explanation.

title: The Mismeasure of Europe’s Economy
summary: While Nobel laureate economists Paul Krugman and Philippe Aghion disagree over how to measure Europe’s productivity gap with the United States, both overlook the value of its accumulated assets. That blind spot obscures the real story: Europe is living off a remarkable inheritance it has yet to convert into new growth.

title: NATO Needs a Defense Market
summary: To convert NATO members' resources into military capabilities, setting defense as a strategic priority, increasing spending, and improving procurement systems are all steps in the right direction. But member countries will need to expand their industrial capacity—and that requires much more.
